profiles.summary: California currently uses notification and response levels for several PFAS as health-based decision context. They are useful for private-well interpretation but are not enforceable private-well compliance standards.

benchmarks.label: PFOA notification level
benchmarks.note: Current response level is 10 ppt.

benchmarks.label: PFOS notification level
benchmarks.note: Current response level is 40 ppt.

benchmarks.label: PFHxS notification level
benchmarks.note: Current response level is 10 ppt.

benchmarks.label: PFHxA notification level
benchmarks.note: Current response level is 10 ppb.

profiles.private_well_use_note: Use California notification and response levels as advisory decision context. Do not present them as private-well compliance findings or as a substitute for California lab and follow-up guidance.

profiles.summary: Massachusetts uses a PFAS6 framework for public-water standards. For private wells, that makes the state route useful, but single-analyte well results should not be casually translated into a PFAS6 determination without the full analyte set.

benchmarks.label: PFAS6 maximum contaminant level
benchmarks.note: PFAS6 refers to six PFAS combined in Massachusetts drinking-water regulation.

profiles.private_well_use_note: Use Massachusetts PFAS6 language carefully. It is helpful for state context, but private-well interpretation still needs the full analyte picture and state lab guidance.

profiles.summary: Maine uses an interim drinking-water standard of 20 ng/L for the sum of six PFAS. That is strong state context for private wells, but the safest Sharp v1 interpretation still treats it as a PFAS6 reference route unless a full PFAS6 result is available.

benchmarks.label: Maine interim PFAS6 standard
benchmarks.note: Maine's interim standard covers PFOA, PFOS, PFNA, PFHpA, PFHxS, and PFDA alone or in combination.

profiles.private_well_use_note: Use Maine's PFAS6 standard as a reference-based route. If a private-well result is above 20 ng/L for PFAS6, Maine CDC materials point households toward reducing consumption, bottled water, and treatment follow-up without presenting a private-well compliance verdict.

profiles.primary_reference_label: Michigan PFAS drinking-water MCLs used with other private-well factors
profiles.summary: Michigan states that private residential wells are not regulated under the Safe Drinking Water Act, but Michigan uses its PFAS MCLs along with other factors when evaluating private residential well results.

benchmarks.label: Michigan MCL for PFOA
benchmarks.note: Useful reference context for private-well interpretation in Michigan.

benchmarks.label: Michigan MCL for PFOS
benchmarks.note: Useful reference context for private-well interpretation in Michigan.

benchmarks.label: Michigan MCL for PFNA
benchmarks.note: Michigan publishes seven PFAS MCLs.

profiles.private_well_use_note: Michigan lets its public-water MCLs inform private-well evaluation, but private wells are still not regulated public-water systems. Keep the output reference-based.

profiles.primary_reference_label: Minnesota health-based guidance and federal PFAS context
profiles.summary: Minnesota has been revising PFAS health-based guidance and explicitly points to both state risk-assessment work and current federal PFAS drinking-water standards. For Sharp v1, Minnesota should be treated as a state-guidance-first route rather than a simple single-number benchmark state.

benchmarks.label: Federal context currently referenced by Minnesota
benchmarks.note: Use as context only; Minnesota guidance should still be read directly before acting.

profiles.private_well_use_note: Minnesota results should be anchored in current MDH guidance and lab context before a household treats a single benchmark line as definitive.

profiles.primary_reference_label: New York PFOA and PFOS MCLs
profiles.summary: New York still requires water systems to meet its state MCLs for PFOA and PFOS until 2029 even after EPA's newer federal PFAS rule. That makes New York a comparatively clear state for PFOA and PFOS private-well context.

benchmarks.label: New York MCL for PFOA
benchmarks.note: New York water systems remain on the 10 ppt state MCL until 2029.

benchmarks.label: New York MCL for PFOS
benchmarks.note: New York water systems remain on the 10 ppt state MCL until 2029.

profiles.private_well_use_note: New York gives a fairly direct PFOA/PFOS reference context, but private wells are still owner-managed and should route through state lab and follow-up guidance.

profiles.summary: Vermont publishes direct numeric MCLs for several PFAS and a grouped hazard-index MCL for mixtures that include PFHxS, PFNA, HFPO-DA, and PFBS. That gives Vermont strong private-well reference context while still requiring treatment and sampling guidance to stay in view.

benchmarks.label: Vermont MCL for PFOA
benchmarks.note: Vermont lists a 4 ppt MCL for PFOA.

benchmarks.label: Vermont MCL for PFOS
benchmarks.note: Vermont lists a 4 ppt MCL for PFOS.

benchmarks.label: Vermont MCL for PFNA
benchmarks.note: Vermont lists a 10 ppt MCL for PFNA.

benchmarks.label: Vermont MCL for PFHxS
benchmarks.note: Vermont lists a 10 ppt MCL for PFHxS.

benchmarks.label: Vermont MCL for HFPO-DA
benchmarks.note: Vermont lists a 10 ppt MCL for HFPO-DA.

benchmarks.label: Vermont grouped PFAS hazard index MCL
benchmarks.note: Applies to mixtures containing two or more of PFHxS, PFNA, HFPO-DA, and PFBS.

profiles.private_well_use_note: Vermont gives direct numeric context for several PFAS, but the household route should still emphasize test-first sampling, pretreatment checks, and the cost difference between point-of-use and whole-home treatment.

profiles.primary_reference_label: Washington PFAS state action levels aligned to federal MCLs
profiles.summary: Washington historically used PFAS state action levels and, effective January 15, 2026, aligned those SAL values with the federal PFAS MCL values while maintaining state monitoring and trigger logic.

benchmarks.label: Washington SAL for PFOA
benchmarks.note: Aligned to federal MCL effective 2026-01-15.

benchmarks.label: Washington SAL for PFOS
benchmarks.note: Aligned to federal MCL effective 2026-01-15.

benchmarks.label: Washington SAL for PFHxS
benchmarks.note: Federal MCL is scheduled at 4 ppt in 2029, but current state SAL is 10 ppt.

benchmarks.label: Washington SAL for PFNA
benchmarks.note: Aligned state action level remains 10 ppt.

benchmarks.label: Washington SAL for HFPO-DA
benchmarks.note: Added as part of Washington's PFAS transition to the federal structure.

profiles.private_well_use_note: Washington private-well interpretation can borrow the current SAL context, but the output should still emphasize state guidance and accredited-lab follow-up rather than imply a public-water compliance result.

profiles.primary_reference_label: Pennsylvania PFAS MCL rule used as private-well reference context
profiles.summary: Pennsylvania adopted PFAS MCLs for PFOA and PFOS in public water, while private wells remain owner-managed. For Sharp v1, Pennsylvania can provide direct numeric reference context for covered analytes, but the route should still keep current state and federal transition notes visible and avoid presenting a private-well compliance determination.

benchmarks.label: Pennsylvania MCL for PFOA
benchmarks.note: Pennsylvania's PFAS MCL rule set PFOA at 14 ppt for public-water compliance context.

benchmarks.label: Pennsylvania MCL for PFOS
benchmarks.note: Pennsylvania's PFAS MCL rule set PFOS at 18 ppt for public-water compliance context.

profiles.private_well_use_note: Use Pennsylvania's PFOA and PFOS MCL values as reference context only. Private wells are not public-water systems, so the household path should stay anchored in accredited-lab interpretation, state guidance, and PFAS-specific follow-up rather than in a compliance-style verdict.

profiles.primary_reference_label: New Jersey PFAS drinking-water standards used with PWTA private-well context
profiles.summary: New Jersey already regulates PFOA, PFOS, and PFNA in drinking water and requires PFAS testing under the Private Well Testing Act. For Sharp v1, New Jersey gives unusually direct private-well reference context for covered analytes, but the route should still keep the current state-to-federal transition and certified-lab workflow visible.

benchmarks.label: New Jersey MCL for PFOA
benchmarks.note: New Jersey currently enforces a 14 ppt state MCL for PFOA while state-federal transition work continues.

benchmarks.label: New Jersey MCL for PFOS
benchmarks.note: New Jersey currently enforces a 13 ppt state MCL for PFOS.

benchmarks.label: New Jersey MCL for PFNA
benchmarks.note: New Jersey currently enforces a 13 ppt state MCL for PFNA and includes PFNA in PWTA-required PFAS testing.

profiles.private_well_use_note: Use New Jersey's PFOA, PFOS, and PFNA standards as reference context for covered analytes and keep PWTA-certified sampling and local follow-up in view. The route should not present a private well as if it were a regulated community system.
